title: Catalog system with system tables and statistics
description: Implement catalog as in-memory cache backed by heap tables. Bootstrap system tables on first run: sys_tables, sys_columns, sys_indexes, sys_constraints, sys_views, sys_statistics (all schemas as specified). Implement catalog operations: create/drop/alter table (updating both heap and cache transactionally), create/drop index, create/drop view, lookup by name/id. Implement ANALYZE command: scan table to compute TableStats (row_count, total_pages) and ColumnStats (null_count, distinct_count, min/max, avg_width, equi-depth histogram with 100 buckets, top-10 most common values with frequencies). Store statistics in sys_statistics. Implement VACUUM: mark dead tuples as reclaimable, update free space map, optional auto-vacuum (threshold + scale factor). Include tests for table/index/view CRUD, DROP CASCADE, concurrent DDL conflict, ANALYZE statistics accuracy (histogram coverage, MCV identification, distinct count), VACUUM space reclamation.
result_summary:
Good - I have a solid picture of the codebase. The project is a from-scratch Rust database engine with a complete storage layer (pages, buffer pool, B+ trees, hash indexes, FSM, TOAST), a full type system with MVCC tuples, and a SQL parser — but **no catalog, execution engine, or statistics yet**. The catalog system is the natural next layer.

Before I dive into the design, one clarifying question:

**Concurrency model for DDL operations**: The task mentions testing "concurrent DDL conflict." The codebase currently has no transaction manager or lock manager implemented (just error types reserved for them). For this catalog implementation, should I:

- **(A)** Use simple `RwLock`-based concurrency on the catalog cache (readers share, DDL writers get exclusive access), with conflict detection returning errors when two DDL ops target the same table — practical and self-contained
- **(B)** Stub out a lightweight locking protocol that a future transaction manager can integrate with (e.g., catalog-level lock table with `LockMode::Shared/Exclusive`)
- **(C)** Keep it single-threaded for now and mock concurrency conflicts in tests only

Which approach fits your vision for how the transaction manager will integrate later?
